_note: Fair-use committed ground truth for the fidelity axis. The FULL book text is NOT included in this repo by design (copyright): supply your own licensed copy at source/book.norm.txt to run the deeper full-book F3 check. attribution: John Ousterhout, A Philosophy of Software Design (2nd ed.)

F2_vocab.asserts: all named_red_flags appear in the committed distillation (source/reference.verified.md)

F3_lightweight.asserts: every confirmed_quote appears in the committed distillation (source/reference.verified.md); verifies the distillation the skill loads carries the grounded quotes

F3_full.asserts: every confirmed_quote is present verbatim in the book AND every fabricated_removed quote is absent from the book; absent book => RECORDED, never a live PASS

confirmed_quotes.quote: This book is about one thing: complexity.
Dealing with complexity is the most important challenge in software design.
The best modules are those whose interfaces are much simpler than their implementations.
Good names have two properties: precision and consistency.
This is tactical programming pure and simple, with all of its disadvantages.
the units of development should be abstractions, not features

fabricated_removed: The most important skill for a software designer is the ability to recognize complexity
Good design is the accumulation of thousands of small decisions
If the interface of a module doesn't tell you what you need to know to use it, then the interface is wrong
You can't design your way out of bad code by adding comments
The whole point of comments is to capture things that can't be expressed in the code
fabricated_note: These passages were quotation-marked as verbatim in the earlier distillation but could NOT be confirmed in the book; they were removed (see FIDELITY-FINDINGS.md). The full-book F3 check asserts they are ABSENT from the book. They are NOT grepped against the distillation, because reference.verified.md self-documents them in its 'removed' note.
principles_checklist: Comments describe what's not obvious from code
Design for ease of reading, not writing
